title: Medical doctors per 1,000 people vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: Medical doctors include generalist physicians and specialist medical practitioners. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
note: GDP per capita is expressed in [international-$](#dod:int_dollar_abbreviation) at 2021 prices.
caption:
Medical doctors per 1,000 people vs. GDP per capita

Medical doctors include generalist physicians and specialist medical practitioners. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot displays the relationship between the number of medical doctors per 1,000 people and GDP per capita for various countries in 2023. The vertical axis measures doctors per 1,000 people, ranging from 0 to over 6, while the horizontal axis represents GDP per capita in international dollars at 2021 prices, plotted on a logarithmic scale from $2,000 to $100,000. Each point represents a country, color-coded by region: North America (orange), South America (maroon), Africa (purple), Europe (blue), Asia (teal), and Oceania (light blue). Circle size corresponds to population, with a reference scale showing sizes for 1.4 billion and 600 million.

European countries generally occupy the upper portion of the chart, with Greece, Portugal, Russia, Austria, Norway, and Germany all showing more than 4 doctors per 1,000 people. Greece appears near the top with approximately 6.5 doctors per 1,000. The United States sits around 3 doctors per 1,000 with a GDP per capita near $70,000. China is represented by a large circle positioned at roughly 3 doctors per 1,000 and a GDP per capita around $20,000. India and Nigeria appear as large circles in the lower-left area, both below 1 doctor per 1,000 people and with GDP per capita under $10,000. Singapore and Brunei show high GDP per capita values exceeding $50,000, with Singapore at approximately 3 doctors per 1,000. African nations including Burundi, DR Congo, and Ethiopia cluster in the bottom-left corner with both low doctor density and low GDP per capita. The visual distribution shows no single linear pattern, with countries spread across the plotting area rather than forming a tight diagonal line.
